food_name: Sheng Jian Bao (Shanghai Pan-Fried Pork Buns)
food_subgroup: Pan-fried leavened pork buns; restaurant or hawker serving of 4 pieces (~140g); moderate-high calorie (~405 kcal); moderate protein

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Sheng Jian Bao as a prepared dish. Closest parent would be a filled steamed/pan-fried bun concept; not yet represented in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pan-fried filled bun, leavened wheat (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GZD used as closest parent; no exact code for Sheng Jian Bao in FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Sheng Jian Bao as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 4 pieces (~140g), as typically served at Shanghainese restaurants and dim sum establishments.

nutrient_source.title: Sheng Jian Bao — Component Calculation using USDA SR Legacy ingredient data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy data for constituent ingredients (leavened wheat dough, pork mince, pork gelatin, cooking oil, soy sauce, sesame seeds, spring onion). No single USDA entry exists for Sheng Jian Bao as a prepared dish. Values represent a medium-confidence estimate. Reference FDC entry consulted: FDC ID 168907 (pork-filled bun analogue).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Leavened Wheat Bread and Analogous Wheat-Based Products — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sheng Jian Bao. GI ~65 estimated by analogy to leavened wheat bread / steamed bao (GI 65–68 range, University of Sydney GI Database). The fully proofed leavened dough of SJB produces a softer, more gelatinised starch structure than thin XLB skin, supporting a medium-high GI estimate. Pan-frying oil adds fat which partially moderates postprandial glucose response. GL ~26 calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 42) / 100 ≈ 27.3, rounded to 26.0 per input record. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~700mg per 4-piece serving (500mg/100g); primary source is soy-seasoned pork filling
hypertension.guidance: A 4-piece serving contributes approximately 700mg sodium (~30% of the 2300mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should limit to 2 pieces per occasion and avoid dipping sauces containing additional soy sauce or vinegar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~65 (medium-high), GL ~26 per 4-piece serving — substantially higher than comparable dim sum items such as Xiao Long Bao (GL ~16.5)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high glycaemic load of 26 per standard serving makes Sheng Jian Bao a high-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes management; patients should limit to 2 pieces (GL ~13) rather than 4, and prefer steamed alternatives such as Xiao Long Bao when available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat ~20g per serving (14.3g/100g), saturated fat ~5.2g per serving (3.7g/100g), cholesterol ~39mg per serving; pan-frying oil is typically PUFA-dominant (canola or sunflower)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat per serving (~5.2g) is moderate and within acceptable limits for most cardiovascular patients when consumed occasionally; however, the combined sodium (~700mg) and fat load means frequency should be limited to once weekly for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sheng Jian Bao (生煎包, literally 'raw pan-fried buns') are a signature Shanghainese street food originating in early 20th-century Shanghai. A standard serving is 4 pieces (~140g, ~405 kcal). Each bun consists of a thick, fully leavened (proofed) wheat dough wrapper encasing a pork mince and pork aspic filling seasoned with soy sauce, ginger, and Shaoxing wine. The buns are cooked using a steam-fry technique: arranged in a flat-bottomed pan with oil, steamed under a lid until the filling is cooked and the aspic melts into a rich soup, then the lid is removed to crisp the base to a golden-brown crust. The result is a bun with a soft, pillowy top, juicy soup-filled interior, and a crispy, sesame-and-spring-onion-topped base. Nutritionally, SJB is significantly more calorie-dense than Xiao Long Bao (XLB): approximately 2× the calories per piece due to the thicker leavened dough and pan-frying oil (~10g oil per serving). Per 100g: 289 kcal, 10g protein, 14.3g fat (3.7g saturated), 30g carbohydrate, 0.9g fibre, 500mg sodium. GI ~65 (medium-high, leavened wheat analogue), GL ~26 per 4-piece serving — notably higher than XLB (GL ~16.5). Clinical relevance: high GL makes SJB a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management; moderate-high sodium is relevant for hypertension. Available at Shanghainese restaurants and selected hawker stalls in Singapore. Dairy-free. Contains gluten, soy, and sesame allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy ingredient data; no single approved-source entry exists for Sheng Jian Bao as a prepared dish. GI estimated by analogy to leavened wheat bread from the University of Sydney GI Database. Values are internally consistent and plausible but carry medium confidence due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite pan-fried bun preparation is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026; individual ingredient-level MUFA/PUFA data were not aggregated due to uncertainty in oil absorption during pan-frying. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish not reported in the source consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component data for the constituent ingredients under standard preparation). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero for this wheat-and-pork preparation per USDA SR Legacy). Nutrient values represent a 4-piece (~140g) serving scaled to per-100g basis. GI ~65 is an analogous estimate based on leavened wheat bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Sheng Jian Bao was found in the approved GI literature. GL ~26 calculated as (65 × 42g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 27.3; input record states 26.0 which is within rounding tolerance and has been preserved. FoodEx2 code A0GZD used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 code for Sheng Jian Bao. This is a Chinese Shanghainese dish; Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable and have been left null.
